root_cause: The node-api /api/flaky endpoint is returning simulated HTTP 500 errors, driving the aggregate error rate to 22–26 errors/s (~48% of all requests). This matches an identical prior incident at 2026-06-29T11:04Z. No downstream dependency (Redis or Postgres) is implicated — both are healthy. The container is stable (no restarts). The fault is application-level: built-in fault-injection logic on the /api/flaky route is firing under live traffic.
title: node-api Active Error Rate Spike — /api/flaky Simulated Failure Recurrence
summary: node-api is experiencing a sustained, active error rate spike of up to 26 errors/s (threshold: 1 err/s), with ~48% of current requests failing. P99 latency is healthy (max 1.5s), and all dependencies (Redis, Postgres) are up and idle. The pattern exactly matches a prior P3 incident caused by the /api/flaky endpoint's built-in simulated-failure logic. The spike is ongoing (last sampled value: 9.33 errors/s). Real user requests are failing at significant scale.

root_cause: Redis 256 MiB maxmemory cap is structurally exhausted by dataset growth. Memory is pinned at ~99.97% (268,341,688 / 268,435,456 bytes). The eviction policy is active but insufficient — evictions peaked at 16.6 keys/s yet memory remains saturated. This is a recurring structural capacity deficit, not a transient spike, now in its 8th consecutive occurrence within approximately 31 hours.
title: Redis Structural OOM – 256 MiB Cap Exhausted (8th Recurrence)
summary: Redis memory is pinned at 99.97% (268.3 MiB / 268.4 MiB maxmemory). This is the 8th consecutive OOM incident on this service, confirming a structural capacity deficit. The eviction policy is firing (peaking at 16.6 keys/s) but is insufficient to reclaim memory and prevent re-saturation after each restart. node-api currently shows low latency and error rate, so upstream blast radius appears contained at this moment, but the risk of cascading impact remains high given the history of prior P1 outages triggered by this condition.

root_cause: Redis 256 MiB maxmemory cap is structurally exhausted by dataset growth. Memory has been pinned at ~99.96% throughout the incident window (min 1.36 MiB only at window start as Redis was just restarting after the prior P1 shutdown, then climbing to 268.3 MiB). The eviction policy is firing (peak ~8.8 keys/s) but is insufficient to prevent the cap from being re-saturated after each restart. This is a recurring structural deficit — the 7th consecutive OOM incident — not a transient spike.
title: Redis Structural OOM – 256 MiB Cap Exhausted (7th Recurrence), Active Evictions Insufficient

root_cause: node-api is not pooling PostgreSQL connections effectively, holding ~85 simultaneous active connections (87% of max_connections=100) against the appdb database. This is a directly recurring incident — the 5th occurrence in 22 hours with an identical signature. The connection pool fix applied after prior incidents was either not deployed to the running container or is not functioning, leaving node-api to open connections without releasing them under normal load.
title: PostgreSQL connection near-exhaustion (88%) — node-api connection pool leak, 5th recurrence in 22h
summary: PostgreSQL connection utilization reached 88% (87/100 connections), breaching the 80% alert threshold. The dominant source is node-api holding ~85 active connections to appdb without effective connection pooling. This is the 5th recurrence of this identical incident in 22 hours; the system is 13 connections from a full connection-refusal outage. No current data loss or query failures, but risk of P1 escalation is high and growing.

root_cause: Redis received a SIGTERM signal at 2026-06-29T11:20:04Z and performed a clean, graceful shutdown (saved final RDB snapshot, then exited). The container is now absent from Docker. The shutdown follows a pattern of 6 consecutive OOM/memory-exhaustion incidents over the past ~32 hours (most recent ~40 minutes prior), strongly suggesting the SIGTERM was sent as part of a restart/remediation attempt or triggered by an orchestrator health-check in response to the ongoing memory crisis. Redis is a DATASTORE with no downstream dependencies; its outage directly impacts node-api (upstream dependent).
title: Redis Down: Graceful Shutdown via SIGTERM — Container Absent, Service Unavailable
summary: Redis is completely down following a graceful SIGTERM-triggered shutdown at 11:20:04 UTC. The container no longer exists. This is the latest event in a series of 6 OOM-related incidents over 32 hours. node-api is currently still running but will degrade as Redis-dependent operations fail. Immediate restart with increased memory allocation is required.

root_cause: The node-api container exited/crashed around 2026-06-29T11:17Z. The process is completely gone — Docker cannot find the container, all live metrics are zero, and Prometheus scrapes return isUp=0. The exact crash trigger is unconfirmed (no fatal/crash-level log found in the available window), but the service stopped emitting logs after 11:16:52Z and never recovered within the investigation window. Prior incidents show a pattern of simulated failures on /api/flaky and hardcoded delays on /api/slow, but those were application-level faults that did not previously cause a full process exit.

Nginx is still running but has no live backend to proxy to, breaking the primary user-facing data path.

root_cause: The /api/slow endpoint in node-api contains a hardcoded artificial delay of approximately 2000ms per request. A volume of 440 requests to this endpoint during the incident window pushed the aggregate P99 above the 2000ms alert threshold. Prometheus histogram interpolation further inflates the reported P99 value (~2.97-2.99s) above the true per-request latency (~2052ms per logs). No downstream dependency (Redis, Postgres) failure is involved.
title: P99 Latency Spike from /api/slow Hardcoded Delay — Recurring Histogram-Inflated Blip

No infrastructure or dependency failure is involved. This is the third identical recurrence today.

root_cause: The /api/slow endpoint in node-api has a hardcoded artificial delay of ~2000ms per request. A burst of 220 requests to this endpoint starting at 11:07:11Z pushed the aggregate P99 latency just above the 2000ms threshold. The histogram bucket interpolation inflates the reported P99 (~2.988s) above the true per-request latency (~2052ms per logs). No infrastructure or dependency failure is involved.

root_cause: The /api/slow endpoint in node-api is executing deliberately slow requests (~3 seconds each). A burst of 425 such requests concentrated around 10:49–10:50 UTC drove the aggregate P99 latency to ~4961–4976ms. This is an application-level slow path — no downstream dependency (Redis, Postgres) shows any stress or degradation.
title: Severe P99 Latency Spike from /api/slow Endpoint (4961ms)
summary: node-api P99 latency spiked to ~4976ms, far exceeding the 2000ms threshold. The root cause is the /api/slow endpoint, which logged 425 requests each completing in ~3001ms during the incident window (10:49–10:50 UTC). Redis and Postgres dependencies are both healthy with no signs of contention. A secondary low-rate error stream from /api/flaky (~0.08 err/s) is an independent intentional fault-injection pattern and is not related to the latency alert.

root_cause: Redis 256 MiB maxmemory cap is structurally exhausted by dataset growth. Memory is pinned at 99.96% (268,338,360 / 268,435,456 bytes). The active eviction policy is firing at ~8.4 keys/s but cannot reclaim memory fast enough to prevent the cap from being saturated. This is a recurring structural capacity deficit — at least the 6th consecutive OOM incident within 24 hours — not a transient spike.
title: Redis Structural OOM – 256 MiB Cap Exhausted (6th Recurrence), Active Evictions Insufficient
summary: Redis memory is pinned at 99.96% of its 256 MiB maxmemory cap with active evictions (~8.4 keys/s) unable to relieve pressure. This is the 6th consecutive OOM incident in under 24 hours, confirming a structural capacity deficit. The eviction policy is active but insufficient for the write throughput. node-api is currently not showing Redis-driven latency impact, but prior incidents demonstrate it can spike to 4+ second P99 latency under this condition.

root_cause: node-api is leaking/not pooling PostgreSQL connections, holding ~85 active connections simultaneously (88% of max_connections=100). Each /api/users request acquires a connection that is not released promptly, exhausting the connection limit. This is a direct recurrence of the P2 incident diagnosed the previous day (2026-06-28T13:35:06Z) with an identical signature.
title: PostgreSQL connection exhaustion (88%) — node-api connection pool leak (recurrence)
summary: PostgreSQL connection near-exhaustion (88/100, 88% utilization) caused by node-api holding ~85 concurrent active connections without effective connection pooling. The condition is active and sustained throughout the 10-minute incident window, with node-api logging continuous DB connection timeouts (~1 per 7 seconds on /api/users) and at least one ECONNREFUSED event at 10:32:15Z indicating postgres briefly hit its connection ceiling. This is a confirmed recurrence of yesterday's P2 incident with the same root cause.

root_cause: PostgreSQL was shut down via an administrator fast-shutdown command (SIGFAST / pg_ctl stop -m fast) at 2026-06-29T10:26:40Z. The container exited cleanly after completing a shutdown checkpoint and has not restarted. This is the root cause of both the SERVICE_DOWN alert on postgres and the HIGH_LATENCY/connection-timeout errors on node-api.
title: PostgreSQL fast shutdown caused DB outage and node-api connection timeouts
summary: PostgreSQL received a fast shutdown request at 10:26:40 UTC on 2026-06-29, terminating all active connections and taking the database offline. The container is currently in a 'down' state with isUp=0. This caused node-api to lose its database connections, resulting in connection timeout errors on /api/users endpoints and elevated latency (P99 ~2540-2948ms) as requests queued and timed out waiting for the DB. The primary data path (node-api → postgres) is broken and the service remains down.
